title: Transitive & Intransitive Verb Pairs — 自動詞・他動詞

meta.focus: Japanese verbs come in transitive/intransitive pairs: 他動詞 (transitive) takes を and has an agent acting on an object; 自動詞 (intransitive) takes が and describes something happening on its own — no external agent needed.

sections.title: One Kanji, Two Verbs
sections.summary: Many Japanese verbs come in pairs that share the same kanji but have different endings. One verb is transitive (他動詞 — someone does something TO something) and the other is intransitive (自動詞 — something happens ON ITS OWN). English often uses the same word for both: 'I start the movie' / 'The movie starts'. Japanese uses two different verbs — and two different particles: を for transitive objects, が for intransitive subjects.
sections.whyItMatters: You already know 出る, 出す, 入る, 入れる, 始まる, and 始める — but did you notice they come in pairs? Understanding WHY each pair exists and which particle to use stops the most common mistake: using the wrong verb or wrong particle. Once you see the transitive/intransitive pattern, you can predict which verb to use even for pairs you haven't studied yet.
youWillLearn: 他動詞 (transitive verbs): [Agent] が [Object] を [Verb] — someone acts on something
自動詞 (intransitive verbs): [Subject] が [Verb] — something happens on its own
The particle difference: を = transitive object; が = intransitive subject
Three complete verb pairs: 出る/出す, 入る/入れる, 始まる/始める
Morphological clues: ～える/ける endings → often transitive; ～まる endings → often intransitive; ～す → almost always transitive
Why 映画が始まる is correct but 映画が始める is wrong

sections.meaning: Transitive verb (他動詞) — someone acts on something
sections.explanation: A transitive verb (他動詞) requires an agent — someone or something performing the action — and a direct object — the thing being acted upon. The object is always marked with を. Think of transitive verbs as 'I [verb] [the thing]': I take out the notes, I put things in the bag, I start the movie. The key question is: Is someone DOING this TO something else? If yes, use the transitive form.
notes: 他動詞 = transitive verb. 他 means 'other' — a transitive verb acts on something OTHER than the subject.
を always marks the direct object: メモを出す (take out the notes), 映画を始める (start the movie), 水を入れる (pour in water).
Transitive verbs need an agent. You cannot say ×映画が始める — the movie cannot 'start itself' with a transitive verb. The agent (the one doing the starting) is missing.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (が = agent — the one taking out)
notes (を = direct object — what is being taken out)
took out (出す — transitive, polite past)

examples.breakdown: Agent (りきぞさんが) + Object (メモを) + Transitive verb (出す). Someone is acting on the notes. を = direct object signal for a transitive verb.

parts.gloss: theater staff (が = agent doing the starting)
movie (を = direct object — what is being started)
started (始める — transitive, polite past)

examples.en: The theater staff started the movie.
examples.breakdown: Agent (映画館の人が) + Object (映画を) + Transitive verb (始める). を before the verb confirms this is transitive — someone is acting on the movie.

sections.meaning: Intransitive verb (自動詞) — something happens on its own
sections.explanation: An intransitive verb (自動詞) describes a change of state or movement that happens WITHOUT an external agent. No one is 'doing it' — it just happens. The subject that undergoes the change is marked with が. Think of intransitive verbs as 'something [verb]s': the movie starts, people enter, the notes come out. The key question is: Is this happening by itself, with no one forcing it? If yes, use the intransitive form.
notes: 自動詞 = intransitive verb. 自 means 'self' — a self-moving, self-changing verb.
が marks the subject that undergoes the change: 映画が始まる (the movie begins — on its own), 人が入る (people enter — on their own).
Intransitive verbs have no direct object — there is no を. If you see を with an intransitive verb, something is wrong.
For movement into a location, に marks the destination (not を): 映画館に入る = enter the theater (destination with に, not object with を).

parts.gloss: movie (が = subject — begins on its own)
started/began (始まる — intransitive, polite past)

examples.breakdown: Subject (映画が) + Intransitive verb (始まる). The movie started by itself — no one 'started it'. が marks the subject that changed state. No を because there is no object.

parts.gloss: many people (が = subject entering on their own)
into the movie theater (に = destination)
entered (入る — intransitive, polite past)

examples.en: Many people entered the movie theater.
examples.breakdown: Subject (たくさんの人が) + Destination (映画館に) + Intransitive verb (入る). People entered on their own. 映画館に (destination に), not 映画館を — there is no direct object.

sections.title: Verb Pairs at a Glance
sections.description: Three transitive/intransitive pairs you already know. Notice how the verb ending and the particle both change.

rows.label: exit / take out
cells: 出る (でる) — to exit, come out
出す (だす) — to take out

rows.label: enter / put in
cells: 入れる (いれる) — to put in

rows.label: begin / start
cells: 始まる (はじまる) — to begin
始める (はじめる) — to start (something)

notes: Intransitive verbs take が for their subject (the thing that changes state) and often に or から for location or source.
Transitive verbs take を for their direct object (the thing being acted upon).
The same kanji appears in both forms — only the verb ending and the particle change.
More pairs unlock later: 集まる/集める (N4.30), 開く/開ける (N4.31), 止まる/止める (N4.35).

sections.meaning: Verb endings that hint at transitive vs intransitive
sections.explanation: Japanese has useful morphological patterns that often (but not always) predict whether a verb is transitive or intransitive. Learning these patterns lets you make educated guesses about new verb pairs you haven't studied yet. Think of these as strong tendencies, not absolute rules — Japanese has exceptions — but they are reliable enough to be very helpful.
notes: ～える / ～ける endings → often TRANSITIVE (他動詞): 始める (start), 入れる (put in). These ichidan verbs tend to have an agent acting on an object.
～まる / ～ある endings → often INTRANSITIVE (自動詞): 始まる (begin), 集まる (gather), 決まる (be decided). These godan verbs tend to describe self-contained changes of state.
～す endings → almost always TRANSITIVE (他動詞): 出す (take out), 直す (fix), 消す (erase). The ～す ending is one of the most reliable transitive signals in Japanese.
～れる endings → often INTRANSITIVE: 壊れる (break/get broken), 離れる (separate). Compare 壊す (to break something) — transitive.
The ichidan/godan pair pattern: ichidan (～える) = transitive, godan (～まる) = intransitive is very common. You will see this again with 集める/集まる (N4.30), 開ける/開く (N4.31), and 止める/止まる (N4.35).

parts.gloss: Rikizo (agent)
notes (を = direct object — the thing being taken out)
took out (出す — ～す ending, almost always transitive, plain past)

examples.breakdown: 出す ends in ～す → almost always transitive. The object (メモを) confirms this — someone acted on the notes.

parts.gloss: movie (が = subject that begins on its own)
started (始まる — ～まる ending, typically intransitive, plain past)

examples.breakdown: 始まる ends in ～まる → typically intransitive. No agent, no を — the movie begins by itself.

sections.title: 始める vs 始まる — Same Kanji, Different Role

items.label: 始まる — Intransitive (自動詞)
points: Something BEGINS on its own — no external agent required
The subject is what changes state: 映画が (the movie begins)
Particle: が marks the subject that starts
No direct object — no を in the sentence

parts.gloss: movie (が = subject — starts on its own)
started (始まる — intransitive, polite past)

items.label: 始める — Transitive (他動詞)
points: An AGENT starts something — external action required
The object is what gets started: 映画を (the movie, as the object)
Particle: を marks the direct object being started
Needs an agent (someone doing the starting)

parts.gloss: theater staff (が = agent doing the starting)
movie (を = direct object — what is being started)
started (始める — transitive, polite past)

example.en: The theater staff started the movie. (they did it)

sections.tip: Same kanji 始, different endings: ～まる = intransitive (it starts by itself), ～める = transitive (someone starts it). When you see を before the verb, you need transitive 始める. When the subject is what begins and there is no を, use intransitive 始まる.

examples.context: 始まる (intransitive) — English class begins on its own

parts.gloss: English class (が = subject — begins on its own)
soon / shortly
will begin (始まる — intransitive, polite present)

examples.note: が marks English class as the subject that begins. No agent, no を — it begins by itself.

examples.context: 始める (transitive) — teacher starts the class

parts.gloss: teacher (が = agent doing the starting)
English class (を = direct object — what the teacher starts)
started (始める — transitive, polite past)

examples.note: を marks English class as the direct object. The teacher (agent) is acting on the class. Compare: 授業が始まった (class started on its own).

examples.context: 入る (intransitive) — people enter on their own

parts.gloss: many people (が = subject entering)
into the theater (に = destination)
entered (入る — intransitive, polite past)

examples.en: Many people entered the movie theater.
examples.note: No agent is forcing people to enter — they enter on their own. に marks the destination; no を because there is no direct object.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (agent — doing the putting)
notes (を = direct object — what is being put in)
into the pocket (に = destination)
put in (入れる — transitive, polite past)

examples.en: Rikizo put his notes in his pocket.
examples.note: を marks the notes as the direct object. Rikizo is the agent placing something somewhere — transitive 入れる.

examples.context: 出る (intransitive) — exiting on one's own

parts.gloss: Rikizo (subject — exits on his own)
from the movie theater (から = source / starting point)
exited (出る — intransitive, polite past)

examples.note: No one is 'sending' Rikizo out — he exits on his own. から marks where he exits from. No を because there is no direct object.

parts.gloss: Rikizo (agent — doing the taking out)
from the pocket (から = source)
notes (を = direct object — what is taken out)
took out (出す — transitive, polite past)

examples.en: Rikizo took his notes out of his pocket.
examples.note: を marks the notes as the object being taken out. Rikizo is acting on the notes — transitive 出す.

sections.title: 映画館で (At the Movie Theater)
sections.context: ゆき and りきぞ are at a movie theater before the film starts.

lines.jp: 映画がすぐ始まりますよ。
そうですね。まだ人が映画館に入っていますね。
lines.en: Right. People are still entering the theater.

lines.jp: りきぞさんはメモを出しましたか。
lines.en: Rikizo, did you take out your notes?
Not yet. I'll take them out now.

lines.jp: 映画館の人が映画を始めましたよ！
lines.en: The theater staff has started the movie!

lines.jp: 映画が終わりました。外に出ましょう。
lines.en: The movie ended. Let's go outside.

lines.jp: 分かりました。メモを中に入れて、出ましょう！
lines.en: Got it. Let me put my notes inside, and let's go!

sections.title: Choose the Correct Verb
sections.instructions: Fill in the blank with the correct verb form. Use the particle clue (が vs を) and the context to decide between the transitive and intransitive pair.

items.explanation: 映画が = the movie (が = subject marker). The movie starts on its own — no external agent. Use the INTRANSITIVE 始まる. 始める (transitive) would be wrong: ×映画が始めます is ungrammatical — 映画 cannot be the agent of a transitive verb acting on itself.

items.translation: The theater staff started the movie.
items.explanation: 映画を = the movie (を = direct object). There is an agent (映画館の人) acting on an object (映画). Use the TRANSITIVE 始める (polite past: 始めました). 始まりました is intransitive and cannot take を: ×映画館の人が映画を始まりました is ungrammatical.

外に = outside (に = direction). Rikizo is exiting on his own — no direct object (を) is present. Use the INTRANSITIVE 出る (polite present: 出ます). 出す (transitive) would need an object: りきぞさんが何かを出す. Without を marking an object, 出す does not fit.

items.before: りきぞさんがメモを中に
items.explanation: メモを = the notes (を = direct object marker). Rikizo is putting something somewhere — he is the agent, and the notes are the object. Use the TRANSITIVE 入れる (polite present: 入れます). 入る (intransitive) cannot take を: ×りきぞさんがメモを中に入ります is ungrammatical.

メモを = the notes (を = direct object). Rikizo is taking the notes out — he is acting ON the notes. Use the TRANSITIVE 出す (polite present: 出します). 出る (intransitive) means 'to exit/come out' and cannot take an object with を. Only the transitive 出す can have を with something being taken out.

sections.title: Transitive & Intransitive Practice
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.explanation: 映画が始まる is correct. 映画が = the movie (が = subject). 始まる is intransitive — something begins on its own. '映画が始める' is wrong: 始める is transitive and needs a human agent acting on an object, not 映画 as the subject. '映画を始まる' is wrong: intransitive 始まる cannot take を. '映画に始める' is wrong on both particle and transitivity.

items.q: Which particle should fill the blank? りきぞさんがメモ___入れました。
items.explanation: 入れる is a transitive verb (他動詞) — it takes を for its direct object. メモを入れました = put the notes in. が marks the subject (not the object here), に marks the destination (where something is put — also used, but comes AFTER を in this sentence), から marks the source. を directly before the verb is the key signal that 入れる is transitive.

items.q: Which verb ending is almost always a signal that the verb is TRANSITIVE?
choices: ～まる (e.g. 始まる, 集まる)
～れる (e.g. 壊れる, 離れる)
items.explanation: ～す endings are almost always TRANSITIVE (他動詞): 出す (take out), 直す (fix), 消す (erase), 話す (speak). The intransitive counterpart often ends in ～れる (壊す/壊れる) or ～まる (始める/始まる). ～まる endings are typically INTRANSITIVE. ～れる endings are typically intransitive (potential or passive-like). ～く covers various verb types with no reliable rule.

items.q: 映画館の人が映画を___。 Which form correctly completes this sentence?
items.explanation: 映画を = the movie (を = direct object). There is an agent (映画館の人) acting on the movie — use TRANSITIVE 始める (polite past: 始めました). 始まりました is intransitive and cannot take を. 入りました/入れました relate to 入る/入れる (enter/put in), which don't fit the meaning 'start the movie'.

items.q: Which Japanese sentence best translates 'The movie ended'?
items.explanation: 映画が終わりました = the movie ended (on its own). 終わる is intransitive — the movie ends by itself, with no agent. が marks the subject that changes state. 映画を終わりました is wrong: を marks a direct object, but intransitive 終わる does not take を. The transitive counterpart 終える (to finish something) takes を — but 終わる does not.
